title: Consistent Hashing - System Design Interview
tagline: Explain and design consistent hashing for scalable caches and distributed storage.
problem: Design a consistent hashing mechanism that assigns keys to servers while minimizing key movement when servers are added or removed.

functional: Map any key to an owning server.
Limit remapping to the affected key range.
Improve load balance with virtual nodes.
Expose ring state for debugging and operations.
nonFunctional: Fast lookup in the request path.
Simple enough to embed in clients or gateways.
Safe metadata rollout during membership changes.
requirements.designPrompt: Use this as a teaching interview: first show why modulo hashing is unstable, then build the ring, then add virtual nodes and rebalancing.

capacity.label: Lookup cost
capacity.value: O(log V) with a sorted token list, where V is virtual node count.

capacity.label: Movement on node add
capacity.value: Only ranges now owned by the new node move.

capacity.label: Virtual nodes per server
capacity.value: Often hundreds, tuned by load balance and metadata size.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: The hash space is large enough that collisions are negligible for placement. Every physical server owns many virtual nodes. Ring metadata is small enough to keep in memory in clients or routing services.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: Capacity is less about raw storage here and more about how much key movement and metadata churn the system creates.

dataModel.name: physical_node
dataModel.note: A real server participating in the ring.

dataModel.name: virtual_node
dataModel.note: A token on the ring owned by a physical node.

dataModel.name: rebalance_task
dataModel.note: Work item for moving keys after membership changes.

steps.title: 1. Show Why Modulo Hashing Breaks
steps.description: The simple formula hash(key) % N remaps many keys whenever N changes. That causes mass cache misses or storage movement.
steps.decisionPrompt: What happens to key ownership when the number of servers changes from N to N+1?

concepts.definition: A placement rule that maps hash(key) to a bucket using remainder by server count.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is simple but unstable during scale-out.

concepts.definition: A key moving from one server to another after membership changes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Large remapping events cause cache misses and data transfer spikes.

deepDives: This is the motivating failure mode: the algorithm is deterministic, but the denominator changes for every key.
talkingPoints: Use a few example keys if the interviewer asks for a concrete demonstration.

steps.title: 2. Put Servers on a Hash Ring
steps.description: Consistent hashing maps both servers and keys into the same circular hash space. Each server owns the range after the previous server and up to its token.
steps.decisionPrompt: How can the placement rule avoid depending on the total number of servers?

concepts.definition: A circular interpretation of a fixed hash space.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets ownership depend on neighboring tokens, not total server count.

concepts.definition: A point in the hash space assigned to a server.
concepts.whyItMatters: Tokens define ownership boundaries.

concepts.definition: The interval of keys assigned to a token owner.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes rebalancing concrete and bounded.

deepDives: The ring is a mental model; in code it is usually a sorted array or tree of tokens.
bottlenecks: Few tokens can still create uneven ranges.
talkingPoints: Clarify that the ring wraps around from the largest token to the smallest token.

steps.title: 3. Look Up the First Server Clockwise
steps.description: A key is assigned to the first token clockwise from the key hash. The lookup can be implemented as a binary search over sorted tokens.
steps.decisionPrompt: Given a key hash, how does the router find the owner quickly?

concepts.definition: Choose the first token greater than or equal to the key hash, wrapping at the end.
concepts.whyItMatters: This is the core consistent hashing lookup rule.

concepts.definition: An ordered in-memory list of tokens.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gives O(log V) lookup without traversing a literal circle.

flows.name: Lookup
flows.note: The request path only needs the ring metadata and a fast binary search.

deepDives: The token list can be embedded in clients for caches or centralized in routing services for storage systems.
bottlenecks: All clients need timely ring metadata updates.

steps.title: 4. Add and Remove Servers With Limited Movement
steps.description: Adding a server only steals ranges from its clockwise successors. Removing a server only moves its ranges to the next clockwise owners.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which keys move when a server joins or leaves the ring?

concepts.definition: The key interval whose owner changes after a token is added or removed.
concepts.whyItMatters: It bounds the data movement.

concepts.definition: A controlled job that copies keys in an affected range.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents membership changes from becoming request-path work.

deepDives: The ideal algorithm reduces movement, but the system still needs a safe copy, verify, and cutover procedure.
bottlenecks: Too many simultaneous rebalances compete with live traffic.
talkingPoints: Say that only neighboring ranges move, not every key.

steps.title: 5. Use Virtual Nodes to Balance Load
steps.description: A physical server owns many virtual nodes. More tokens smooth uneven hash ranges and allow heavier servers to own more tokens.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we avoid one physical server getting a much larger ring segment than another?

concepts.definition: A token that maps back to a physical server.
concepts.whyItMatters: Many virtual nodes reduce imbalance.

concepts.definition: Assigning more virtual nodes to larger servers.
concepts.whyItMatters: It handles heterogeneous capacity.

concepts.definition: Spreading ownership so one server does not receive a disproportionate share.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves p99 latency and capacity use.

deepDives: Virtual nodes are the standard answer to the uneven-partition criticism of basic consistent hashing.
bottlenecks: More virtual nodes increase metadata size and rebalance task count.
talkingPoints: Mention that capacity weights can be changed gradually.

steps.title: 6. Rebalance Affected Ranges Safely
steps.description: When ownership changes, the system copies the affected key range, verifies it, updates routing metadata, and later cleans old copies.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we move keys without losing reads or writes during the transition?

concepts.definition: Temporarily checking old and new owners during migration.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids missing keys while ownership is changing.

concepts.definition: A monotonically increasing ring configuration version.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets clients and servers reason about stale routing information.

concepts.definition: Controlling how much rebalance work runs at once.
concepts.whyItMatters: It protects production traffic.

deepDives: Consistent hashing limits how much moves; it does not eliminate the need for migration safety.
bottlenecks: Stale clients may keep writing to old owners.
talkingPoints: A practical answer includes versioned metadata.

steps.title: 7. Integrate the Ring Into a Cache or Store
steps.description: In production, consistent hashing is a placement component. It is paired with health checks, metadata distribution, observability, and sometimes replication.
steps.decisionPrompt: What extra pieces are needed to use consistent hashing safely in a real system?

concepts.definition: The authority for active nodes and token assignments.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents every client from inventing its own ring.

concepts.definition: A signal that a node can serve traffic.
concepts.whyItMatters: It informs routing and replacement decisions.

concepts.definition: Choosing additional clockwise distinct physical servers after the primary owner.
concepts.whyItMatters: It extends the same ring idea to replicated storage.

deepDives: Systems like Dynamo, Cassandra, Akamai, and load balancers use variants of this idea because it localizes change.
bottlenecks: Split-brain ring metadata can route writes inconsistently.
Failed nodes need a policy: skip, replace, or serve stale.
talkingPoints: Close by restating the win: stable placement under membership change.

functional.how: The ring lookup depends on neighboring tokens rather than server count, so node changes move only affected key ranges.
Routers keep a sorted token list and use binary search to find the first clockwise owner.
Many virtual nodes per physical server smooth token ranges and allow capacity-weighted placement.
Versioned metadata, range movement tasks, fallback reads, health checks, and metrics make ownership transitions operationally safe.

nonFunctional.how: Adding capacity creates bounded range movement instead of invalidating the whole keyspace.
Ring versions and controlled rebalancing prevent stale routers and backfills from corrupting live traffic.
Weighted virtual-node assignment lets larger servers own more of the hash space.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design is a reusable placement subsystem. A membership service owns versioned ring metadata, each physical server owns many virtual nodes, routers binary-search the sorted token list to find the first clockwise owner, optional replica walks choose additional distinct nodes, and controlled rebalance tasks move only affected ranges after membership changes.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through a key lookup, then add one server and explain exactly which keys move and how traffic remains safe during migration.

concepts.definition: Every token assignment is published as a ring version.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets clients, routers, and servers reason about stale placement information.

concepts.definition: The interval whose owner changes when a token is added or removed.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the core benefit over modulo hashing.

concepts.definition: Physical servers own many tokens, often proportional to capacity.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves balance and supports heterogeneous machines.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

options.title: Central router variant
options.description: Central routers simplify metadata rollout and observability, while client-side lookup removes one hop. Both use the same ring model.

flows.name: Key lookup
flows.note: The request path is a hash, binary search, optional replica walk, and route.

messages.label: owner virtual node and physical node

flows.name: Add node and rebalance
flows.note: Adding a token creates bounded range movement with versioned metadata and temporary fallback reads.

deepDives: Consistent hashing is not a whole database. It is the placement and movement mechanism used inside caches, storage systems, and load balancers.
bottlenecks: Too few virtual nodes create imbalance.
Stale ring metadata can route requests to old owners.
Rebalancing can compete with production traffic.
talkingPoints: Say binary search over tokens, not literal ring traversal.
Mention virtual nodes before claiming balanced load.

followUps: How many virtual nodes would you choose and why?
How do you roll out ring metadata safely?
How does replication change the lookup algorithm?
What happens when a node is slow but not fully down?

links.why: Canonical production design for partitioning, replication, hinted handoff, vector clocks, and tunable consistency.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Redis Cluster Specification
links.why: Covers hash slots, failover, and operational constraints for sharded cache-like systems.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Cassandra: A Decentralized Structured Storage System
links.why: Useful for high-write distributed storage, eventual consistency, and wide-column message histories.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Scaling Memcache at Facebook
links.why: Production cache architecture with regional pools, invalidation, leases, and hot-key handling.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: The Tail at Scale
links.why: Explains why p99 latency dominates large fanout systems and motivates hedging, caching, and bounded work.

api.description: Return the owner and replicas for a key.
Add a physical server and allocate virtual nodes.
Remove a server and mark ranges for movement.
Inspect ring tokens and range ownership.
